title: Telemachus versus Mentor
content:
آ آ آ آ Donï؟½t mind me, I beg you, old fellow, Iï؟½ll do very well here alone;
آ آ آ آ You must not be kept from your ï؟½Germanï؟½ because Iï؟½ve dropped in like a stone.
آ آ آ آ Leave all ceremony behind you, leave all thought of aught but yourself;
آ آ آ آ And leave, if you like, the Madeira, and a dozen cigars on the shelf.

آ آ آ آ As for me, you will say to your hostess well, I scarcely need give you a cue.
آ آ آ آ Chant my praise! All will list to Apollo, though Mercury pipe to a few.
آ آ آ آ Say just what you please, my dear boy; thereï؟½s more eloquence lies in youthï؟½s rash
آ آ آ آ Outspoken heart-impulse than ever growled under this grizzling mustache.

آ آ آ آ Go, don the dress coat of our tyrant, youthï؟½s panoplied armor for fight,
آ آ آ آ And tie the white neckcloth that rumples, like pleasure, and lasts but a night;
آ آ آ آ And pray the Nine Gods to avert you what time the Three Sisters shall frown,
آ آ آ آ And youï؟½ll lose your high-comedy figure, and sit more at ease in your gown.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s off! Thereï؟½s his foot on the staircase. By Jove, what a bound! Really now
آ آ آ آ Did I ever leap like this springald, with Loveï؟½s chaplet green on my brow?
آ آ آ آ Was I such an ass? No, I fancy. Indeed, I remember quite plain
آ آ آ آ A gravity mixed with my transports, a cheerfulness softened my pain.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s gone! Thereï؟½s the slam of his cab door, thereï؟½s the clatter of hoofs and the wheels;
آ آ آ آ And while he the light toe is tripping, in this armchair Iï؟½ll tilt up my heels.
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s gone, and for what? For a tremor from a waist like a teetotum spun;
آ آ آ آ For a rosebud thatï؟½s crumpled by many before it is gathered by one.

آ آ آ آ Is there naught in the halo of youth but the glow of a passionate race
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Midst the cheers and applause of a crowd to the goal of a beautiful face?
آ آ آ آ A race that is not to the swift, a prize that no merits enforce,
آ آ آ آ But is won by some faineant youth, who shall simply walk over the course?

آ آ آ آ Poor boy! shall I shock his conceit? When he talks of her cheekï؟½s loveliness,
آ آ آ آ Shall I say ï؟½twas the air of the room, and was due to carbonic excess?
آ آ آ آ That when waltzing she drooped on his breast, and the veins of her eyelids grew dim,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas oxygenï؟½s absence she felt, but never the presence of him?

آ آ آ آ Shall I tell him first love is a fraud, a weakling thatï؟½s strangled in birth,
آ آ آ آ Recalled with perfunctory tears, but lost in unsanctified mirth?
آ آ آ آ Or shall I go bid him believe in all womankindï؟½s charm, and forget
آ آ آ آ In the light ringing laugh of the world the rattlesnakeï؟½s gay castanet?

آ آ آ آ Shall I tear out a leaf from my heart, from that book that forever is shut
آ آ آ آ On the past? Shall I speak of my first love Augusta my Lalage? But
آ آ آ آ I forget. Was it really Augusta? No. ï؟½Twas Lucy! No. Mary! No. Di!
آ آ آ آ Never mind! they were all first and faithless, and yet Iï؟½ve forgotten just why.

آ آ آ آ No, no! Let him dream on and ever. Alas! he will waken too soon;
آ آ آ آ And it doesnï؟½t look well for October to always be preaching at June.
آ آ آ آ Poor boy! All his fond foolish trophies pinned yonder a bow from her hair,
آ آ آ آ A few billets-doux, invitations, and whatï؟½s this? My name, I declare!

آ آ آ آ Humph! ï؟½Youï؟½ll come, for Iï؟½ve got you a prize, with beauty and money no end:
آ آ آ آ You know her, I think; ï؟½twas on dit she once was engaged to your friend;
آ آ آ آ But she says thatï؟½s all over.ï؟½ Ah, is it? Sweet Ethel! incomparable maid!
آ آ آ آ Or what if the thing were a trick? this letter so freely displayed!

آ آ آ آ My opportune presence! No! nonsense! Will nobody answer the bell?
آ آ آ آ Call a cab! Half past ten. Not too late yet. Oh, Ethel! Why donï؟½t you go? Well?
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Master said you would waitï؟½ Hang your master! ï؟½Have I ever a message to send?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Yes, tell him Iï؟½ve gone to the German to dance with the friend of his friend.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
